data.title: QDVO: Amplify Your Retirement Income With This ETF
data.description:
Overview

Traditional investment advice encourages folks to follow the 4% rule: invest in an index fund and withdraw 4% to support your lifestyle expenses. The issue here is that many investors do not have a sizeable nest egg built. So when using this 4% withdrawal rate, the annual income retirees are stuck with can be insufficient. New options-based funds, such as the Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF (QDVO), can provide a double-digit yield for investors seeking a high level of sustainable income. Furthermore, QDVO still provides this high yield while still offering direct exposure to some of the highest-quality businesses in the world. This makes QDVO a great alternative for investors that aren't fond of high-yield credit funds that rely on healthy debt markets.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that QDVO's share price increased by nearly 16.8%. The fund has even demonstrated its ability to participate in the recent market recovery over the last few weeks. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, QDVO's total return jumps up above 29.3% over the same time frame. The fund now offers investors a starting dividend yield of ~10.7%, while the distributions are paid out on a monthly basis. However, the payout amounts consistently change, so the estimated yield can vary based on market conditions.

When I previously covered QDVO, I issued a buy rating because of the potential to collect tax-efficient income and capital appreciation over a longer holding period. While these things are still true, I wanted to revisit the fund to provide some insight into why the fund can be a great option for retirees seeking stability and capital preservation. While the fund's higher yield does come with some more risk than traditional ETFs, I believe the elevated risk profile is worth it for retirees looking to grow their wealth and produce a monthly income that is large enough to support lifestyle expenses.

Fund Strategy

Since the time of my last coverage, QDVO has continued to grow in popularity. For instance, QDVO now has total net assets of $616.6M, which is more than a 50% increase from its net assets of $409.3M. The data suggests that there is a clear demand for funds that can provide a high income without totally eliminating exposure to the equity markets. For instance, equity income ETFs attracted $97B of inflows over 2025, which was a record level. The fund takes a very active approach and only has exposure to 47 positions at this time.

The fund doesn't try to mimic any specific index, so investors are reliant on management's selection process. Since QDVO is actively managed, the expense ratio sits at 0.56% of assets, which is higher than more traditional index-tracking ETFs. However, this higher management fee is worth it considering the high yield paid out to investors. The fund aims to provide attractive growth and total returns, so it prioritizes the following traits when constructing its portfolio:

  • Large-cap growth equities with a strong earnings history and cash flow characteristics.
  • Provides balance across sectors, while shifting allocation based on market conditions.
  • Limits holdings to companies that have favorable relative performance in their industry and a positive management track record.

As you may already know, there aren't many equities that provide a double-digit dividend yield. So to support the large distributions, QDVO utilizes a strategic covered call option writing strategy against its holdings. The fund's option writing strategy is dynamically adjusted based on market conditions. QDVO can write calls on anywhere between 30% and 50% of its assets while specifically utilizing an OTM (out-of-the-money) strategy that allows for some upside growth since the selected strike price is usually above the current market price of an asset. So during strong bull markets, QDVO may write fewer options so that its portfolio can participate in more upside growth.

Since the fund's distribution is so generous, part of QDVO's strategy means that it will need to sell some of the upside growth of its positions at times. Therefore, it comes as no surprise that QDVO is most heavily allocated to technology investments at 45.41% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to communication and consumer cyclical investments, which accounts for 16.15% and 11.99% of assets, respectively.

Since QDVO is actively managed, there is no set rebalancing schedule. Instead, investors are relying on management to stay proactive with its positioning. However, I think this works out to QDVO's benefit so that the fund isn't locked into any underperforming positions until the end of a quarter. Nvidia (NVDA) is currently the largest holding at 11.13% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to other notable companies, including Apple (AAPL), Alphabet (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), or Broadcom (AVGO), just to name a few. So an investment income QDVO means that investors are getting direct exposure to some of the highest quality businesses in the world.

Weakness

When assessing an option ETF, it should be understood that these funds are not structured to keep up with the indices, such as the S&P 500 (SPX) or Nasdaq-100 (NDX). While QDVO has many of the same equities that make up the indices, the inclusion of an option strategy means that upside growth is extremely limited in comparison. The fund is essentially sacrificing future growth potential for current income generation, and this is exactly why QDVO is best utilized for retirees above anyone else. Retirees may be the only class of investor that can accept underperformance against the indices in order to prioritize a high-income or capital preservation.

Furthermore, the fund's high-yield and monthly distributions don't necessarily translate to outperformance during periods of uncertainty. While the high distributions can serve as a slight buffer during market pullbacks, it ultimately depends on how rapid of a decline is experienced. While the upside growth is capped, the downside risks are not. For instance, market indices have been trading sideways over the last six months.

Since QDVO is unable to capture any net realized gains during flat markets, the fund is reliant on net investment income and option premiums received to support distributions. The reality is that sometimes the fund isn't capable of producing sufficient income, so the share price may deteriorate. This issue is more relevant during rapid pullbacks, like we've experienced through early April. Since the fund's potential to recover from these pullbacks is limited, this is where the underperformance is likely to be relevant. For instance, QDVO's total return over the last six months was 2.54%, which underperforms against State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) over the same time frame.

Furthermore, the limited upside growth also means that the fund will struggle to recover from prolonged or rapid market declines. While it is certainly possible for the fund to eventually recover, it basically relies on an extended rally. For instance, market indices recovered in recent weeks, but the fund's share price is still down by nearly 2.2%, while SPY and QQQ have both recovered over the last six months.

Outlook

I think that QDVO can be a solid middle ground for investors that are bullish on the growth of AI and technology, but believe that the future will be volatile. I am very optimistic about the growth of different technology sectors, but I think that there will be periods of volatility. This volatility can be great for QDVO since the fund will be able to capture a higher level of premiums from its positions. For instance, the market indices have trended sideways due to uncertainty from record-breaking capex spending levels from the hyperscalers.

So who is QDVO best aligned for? I think QDVO is best aligned for the optimistic investor that thinks long-term returns of the market will be solid but the road forward will be rocky. Since QDVO is most heavily allocated to the technology sector, it will have the greatest impact on QDVO's price growth and total return potential. Based on QDVO's positions, the fund is directly positioned to participate in the expansion of the digital transformation market. This is the market that represents general integration of technology into all areas of business, which changes how companies can create and deliver value to consumers.

Grand View Research estimates that the size of the digital transformation market can grow to a value of ~$4.6T by the end of 2030. Starting from 2025, this represents a potential CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 28.5% over the next few years. Even if this estimate is off by a 50% margin, this would still indicate a potential double-digit upside growth.

QDVO is directly aligned to participate in this expansion through its equity positions. So over the long term, QDVO is great for retirees because it allows you to still participate in market growth. I always hated the idea of selling shares to fuel your retirement because it meant you were actively reducing your ability to participate in the expansion of the market. By relying on QDVO to generate distributions instead, you aren't forced to sell shares and can still participate in the positive market momentum over time.

So despite the fund's structural limitations, QDVO can be a fund that preserves capital for you. I like the idea of living off distributions instead of selling shares, because it means that your portfolio can be passed on to your family. QDVO makes the idea of retirement a lot easier to manage compared to the 4% rule.

Dividend Health

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.2445 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 10.7%. Since distributions are supported by the combination of option premiums, dividends received, and capital gains, the payouts will vary month to month. Despite the variability of distributions, QDVO has done a great job at keeping payouts pretty consistent. The only thing I would caution here is that the payouts are likely to decline during periods where market indices are trending lower.

As the market indices pulled back from their all-time highs over the last few months, we can see below how QDVO's distribution amounts have also trended slightly lower. Although the fund is paying out a large yield, QDVO isn't overextending itself with its distributions. By preserving some of its earnings during favorable market periods, the fund has some excess income that can be tapped into. So when it comes to the utility for retirees, QDVO is a great option for investors seeking stability and reliability, despite the slight adjustments in payouts.

We can get confirmation of this when assessing the most up-to-date annual report for 2025. For instance, the fund experienced a net increase in net assets from options of $21.1M. This growth was mostly fueled by the combination of net investment income and a positive net change in unrealized appreciation. Meanwhile, the fund only paid out a total of $9.05M over the same time frame. Therefore, the fund's payouts are quite reasonable, and it is clear that QDVO will be able to support distributions through periods of positive market momentum. And even when markets are trading sideways or downward, QDVO has the ability to create a buffer from prior years to support those distributions.

However, QDVO is a type of fund where investors should not anticipate any sort of traditional dividend growth over time. While it is certainly possible for payouts to trend higher during prolonged bull markets, the income experience with QDVO is far from traditional. Despite this, the fund's ability to keep payouts within a consistent range still makes it a great tool for income compounders. Consistent payouts enable investors to create their own dividend growth through consistent reinvestment or by adding new capital over time.

In my opinion, I believe it makes the most sense to implement a dividend wheel strategy with QDVO. By this, I mean to manually reinvest those distributions back into different areas of your portfolio. While QDVO is capable of providing capital appreciation, the limited upside potential means that QDVO's distributions aren't the most efficient when reinvested. Instead, I, personally, use the distributions from high-yield funds to manually reinvest in different growth positions within my portfolio. This way, I am accumulating shares of high-quality growth ETFs or stocks without using my own capital. However, this only matches my specific circumstances and goals, so this approach isn't necessary if you are a retiree simply looking to generate extra spending income.

Lastly, the fund continues to issue tax-efficient distributions by utilizing return of capital. The latest section19(a) notice indicates that 97% of the distributions paid YTD were classified as return of capital, which isn't considered to be income and therefore isn't taxed as such. Instead, return of capital distributions directly reduce an investor's cost basis and allow taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. But if you are a retiree that doesn't have any plans to sell a position, you can indefinitely defer your taxes until your cost basis reaches $0. At this point, all distributions would be classified as long-term capital gains, which still counts as qualified dividend income and has favorable tax treatment.

The caveat is that tax-efficient distributions don't necessarily mean 100% tax-free. 3% of the payouts YTD were still classified as being supported by net investment income, which has the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, investors should still anticipate that there may be some tax impact when held inside a traditional brokerage account, even if it is minimal.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on QDVO. The fund is directly aligned to participate in the growth of the digital transformation market while providing a high dividend yield with ease. The fund does have some structural limitations when it comes to price appreciation, but this is to be expected with option ETFs. QDVO is great for retirees because of its strong track record, consistent payouts, and ability to participate in the expansion of the market over a longer holding period. QDVO's dynamic option writing strategy also means that the fund is unlikely to implement an aggressive option writing strategy during strong market rallies, which means that it can provide capital appreciation and higher income levels.
